name: Geb
shortDescription: Egyptian god of the Earth, son of Shu and Tefnut, often depicted lying beneath the sky goddess Nut.
description: Geb, in Egyptian mythology, is the god of the Earth. He is the son of Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture), and the husband and brother of Nut (the sky). Geb is typically depicted as a man lying horizontally, often green-skinned to symbolize fertility, with Nut arched above him, supported by Shu. His separation from Nut, orchestrated by Ra, created the space for the world to exist. Geb is considered the father of Osiris, Isis, Set, and Nephthys, making him a key figure in the Osiris myth cycle. As the Earth, he is the source of all plant life and minerals, and his laughter was believed to cause earthquakes. He was also seen as a wise and just ruler, eventually passing his kingship to Osiris. His association with royalty and the land made him a powerful symbol of earthly authority.
subtitle: Earthly Ruler, Father of Gods, Grounded Divinity
achievements: Separation of Earth and Sky (with Shu)
Fathering Osiris, Isis, Set, and Nephthys
Ruling as King of the Gods on Earth
Being the source of plant life and minerals
Passing kingship to Osiris, ensuring divine order
weapons: His own body (as the Earth itself)
quests: Ensuring the fertility of the land
Maintaining the balance between Earth and sky
Passing on his kingship to the next generation of gods

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Geb's origin story places him as the son of Shu, the god of air, and Tefnut, the goddess of moisture. He and his sister Nut were deeply entwined, so much so that their father Shu had to separate them to create space for the world. This separation is a central theme in Egyptian cosmology. Geb's role in the Osiris myth is also significant; he is the father of Osiris, Isis, Set, and Nephthys, all pivotal figures in the Egyptian pantheon. His connection to the land made him a symbol of stability and grounding. Myths portray him as both a benevolent ruler and a vulnerable god, experiencing joy (earthquakes) and pain (natural disasters).

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Geb's role in Egyptian religion was multifaceted. He was not only the god of the Earth but also a symbol of kingship and fertility. Pharaohs were often associated with Geb, inheriting their right to rule from him. As the Earth, he was responsible for the growth of crops and the provision of resources, making him essential for the survival of the Egyptian people. Temples were dedicated to him, and offerings were made to ensure the continued fertility of the land. He was also considered a wise and just judge, resolving disputes among the gods and mortals. His association with the afterlife is evident in funerary texts, where he is depicted as welcoming the deceased into the earth.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Geb symbolizes the Earth in its physical and metaphorical sense. His green skin represents fertility and growth, while his horizontal position signifies the grounded nature of the Earth. The separation of Geb and Nut symbolizes the creation of the world, separating the earthly realm from the celestial. He represents the stability and foundation upon which life is built. His role as a father figure reinforces the idea of the Earth as a nurturing provider. The earthquakes associated with his laughter can be interpreted as both a destructive and creative force, reflecting the Earth's capacity for both. He embodies the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Geb's legacy continues to resonate in modern understandings of Egyptian mythology. He remains a prominent figure in scholarly studies of ancient Egyptian religion and culture. His image, often depicted on temple walls and in tombs, is a recognizable symbol of Egyptian art. His role in the Osiris myth cycle ensures his continued relevance in popular culture depictions of ancient Egypt. The concept of the Earth as a divine being, embodied by Geb, has influenced ecological and environmental perspectives, highlighting the importance of respecting and preserving the natural world. He serves as a reminder of the interconnectedness between humanity and the Earth.

sources.author: R.T. Rundle Clark
sources.work: Myth and Symbol in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Provides a detailed analysis of Egyptian myths and their symbolic meanings.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of the Egyptian pantheon, including Geb's role and attributes.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: Geb IS the Earth, its physical manifestation and divine embodiment.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Represents fertility, vegetation, and the life-giving properties of the Earth.

associations.name: Goose
associations.significance: Associated with Geb, sometimes depicted with a goose on his head.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Gaia
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Represents the Earth and is considered the mother of all life.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Terra Mater
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Earth goddess and personification of the Earth.

companions.name: Nut
companions.description: The sky goddess and Geb's sister/wife, separated from him by Shu.

companions.name: Shu
companions.description: God of air, Geb's father, who separated him from Nut.

companions.name: Osiris
companions.description: God of the afterlife, eldest son of Geb and Nut.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Farmers would offer crops and minerals to Geb to ensure a bountiful harvest.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Priests would perform rituals in Geb's temples to maintain the Earth's fertility and stability.

festivals.name: Harvest Festivals
festivals.description: Celebrated the bounty of the Earth and gave thanks to Geb for the harvest.

cultural.modernLegacy: Geb's enduring presence in art, literature, and academic studies ensures his continued influence on our understanding of ancient Egyptian culture. His association with the Earth makes him relevant to modern environmental awareness.
